title: Folding envelopes from handmade sheets
subtitle: Cutting and folding your own paper into functional envelopes
excerpt: A handmade sheet becomes a far more personal object when it arrives as its own envelope. The classic diamond fold requires no template for a standard letter size and uses nearly the full sheet with almost no waste. This tutorial covers the fold, the gluing sequence, and the sizing considerations for A5 and A6 inserts.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Papermaking: The History and Technique of an Ancient Craft (1943, public domain via Internet Archive), on the use of handmade sheets for correspondence and stationery.

glossaryTerms.definition: An envelope-folding method in which a square or near-square sheet is oriented as a diamond (point up) and the four corners are folded inward to form the envelope body, side flaps, and closing flap.
A compressed crease made with a bone folder and ruler before folding. Scoring against the grain of the paper prevents cracking along the fold edge.
The topmost flap of an envelope, folded down to seal the contents. The closure flap is left unglued during construction and sealed by the sender.

content.text: envelope is the most efficient use of a single handmade sheet. Oriented at 45 degrees, it folds into a clean rectangular pocket with three glued flaps and one
. For a handmade sheet, the deckle edge on the top flap adds a decorative quality that machine-cut envelopes cannot replicate.

items.name: Handmade sheet, approximately 22 x 22 cm
items.notes: A square or near-square sheet for a diamond fold. For an A6 insert (105 x 148 mm), a 22 cm square produces a well-proportioned envelope.

items.name: Bone folder
items.notes: For scoring and sharpening each fold.

items.name: Metal ruler, 30 cm
items.notes: For scoring with the bone folder.

items.name: Pencil
items.notes: For marking fold lines before scoring.

items.name: Glue stick
items.notes: For gluing the side and base flaps. Applies dry without wrinkling lightweight handmade paper.

content.text: Lay the sheet as a diamond, point at the top. Mark the centre point lightly with a pencil. From the centre, measure out and mark the insert width divided by two to each side: this sets the width of the envelope pocket. The left and right corner flaps will fold in to meet these marks. Fold the bottom point up to the centre mark to form the base of the pocket.
Score each fold line with the bone folder and ruler before folding.

is especially important on handmade paper, which may be thicker and more fibrous than commercial stock and can crack along an unscored fold. Fold the base flap up first, then fold in the left and right flaps, then check that the pocket width matches the intended insert before gluing.

Apply glue stick to the inner face of the base flap, then fold the left and right flaps over it and press firmly. Place under a light weight for ten minutes. Leave the closure flap unglued. A deckle-edged closure flap is a feature of handmade stationery: do not trim it to a sharp edge.

items.symptom: The pocket is too narrow for the intended insert
items.cause: The side flaps were folded too far in.
items.fix: Measure and mark the pocket width before folding the side flaps. The insert should slide in with 3 to 5 mm clearance on each side.

items.symptom: The fold lines crack or show a rough edge
items.cause: The paper was folded without scoring.
items.fix: Always score handmade paper with a bone folder and ruler before folding. Handmade sheets are often thicker and less consistently made than machine paper and need a scored line to fold cleanly.
